news_summary: Physicists have successfully recreated black hole energy extraction in the lab, marking a breakthrough in understanding and harnessing cosmic phenomena.
news_summary_fr: Des physiciens ont réussi à reproduire en laboratoire le processus d'extraction d'énergie d'un trou noir, ce qui marque une avancée majeure dans la compréhension et l'exploitation des phénomènes cosmiques.
news_summary_es: Los físicos han logrado recrear en el laboratorio la extracción de energía de un agujero negro, lo que supone un gran avance en la comprensión y el aprovechamiento de los fenómenos cósmicos.

articles.title: Physicists recreate black hole energy extraction in the lab
articles.summary: Researchers have recreated the physics of extracting energy from a spinning black hole using a stationary device that produces synthetic ultrafast rotation. The achievement transforms a long-standing theoretical idea into a practical experiment and could inspire new advances in optics, wireless communications, and quantum science.
articles.content:
More than 50 years ago, physicist Sir Roger Penrose proposed a remarkable idea: under the right conditions, it might be possible to extract energy from a rapidly spinning black hole. In his concept, a particle entering the black hole's ergosphere, a region where spacetime is dragged along by the object's rotation, could split into two. One fragment would fall into the black hole while the other escaped carrying away more energy than the original particle. Later, physicist Yakov Zel'dovich expanded on this concept, predicting that waves interacting with an object rotating fast enough could also gain energy and become amplified.

Now, researchers at the Advanced Science Research Center at the CUNY Graduate Center (CUNY ASRC) have demonstrated an experimental approach inspired by those long standing theories. Writing in the journal Nature, the team showed that wave amplification can be achieved using a device that simulates extreme rotation without physically spinning.

Synthetic Rotation Recreates Extreme Physics

Instead of rotating an object mechanically, the researchers built a radio frequency device whose properties are rapidly changed across both space and time. This carefully engineered system creates the illusion of ultrafast rotation, reaching effective rotational speeds far beyond what conventional mechanical systems can achieve. By replacing physical motion with synthetic rotation, the researchers overcame challenges that have limited experimental studies of extreme rotational physics for decades.

"Our approach facilitates a new method of wave-matter interaction in which waves with selected rotational properties extract energy from synthetic time-engineered rotation, producing a form of broadband selective amplification," said principal investigator Andrea Alù, Distinguished Professor and Einstein Professor of Physics at the CUNY Graduate Center and founding director of the CUNY ASRC's Photonics Initiative.

Lead author Hadiseh Nasari, a post-doctoral researcher with the CUNY ASRC's Photonics Initiative, said the experiment transforms a long standing theoretical concept into a practical research tool.

"This successful experiment moves ideas about extreme rotational dynamics from theory to practice and creates a versatile experimental platform for exploring a broad range of phenomena at the intersection of astrophysics, wave physics, and quantum science," said Nasari. "The work has implications for advances in fundamental science and in communications, optics and photonics."

How the Experiment Worked

The researchers set out to answer a fundamental question: Could electromagnetic waves interacting with a completely stationary device behave as though they were encountering an object rotating at ultrafast speed and draw energy from that synthetic motion?

To investigate, they constructed a ring of electronic resonators whose properties were rapidly adjusted in a carefully synchronized sequence. Although the hardware itself never moved, these timed changes generated a traveling pattern around the ring. As a result, the electromagnetic waves effectively experienced the system as though it were spinning at extraordinary speed.

"Waves with the appropriate rotational characteristics extracted energy from the system and became amplified, reproducing the essential physics of the Penrose-Zel'dovich process," said co-lead author Hady Moussa, a former PhD student with the CUNY ASRC Photonics Initiative. "Our approach relies on engineered metamaterials that are designed to control how waves propagate."

Potential Applications Beyond Black Hole Physics

Because synthetic rotation can imitate motion beyond the speed of light, researchers now have a controlled laboratory platform for exploring physical regimes that would otherwise be impossible to study directly. The work creates new opportunities for investigating extreme physics while also pointing toward future advances in wireless communications, optics, photonics, and quantum technologies.

The researchers note that additional work will be needed before these ideas can be translated into practical devices. They also believe the same principles could be applied to photonic and quantum systems, opening new possibilities for controlling light, processing information, and studying wave behavior inspired by some of the universe's most extreme environments.

The research was supported by the U.S. Department of Defense, the U.S. National Science Foundation, and the Simons Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant experimental breakthrough where physicists recreated the energy extraction process from spinning black holes in a lab using synthetic ultrafast rotation. This achievement transforms a long-standing theoretical concept into a practical experimental platform with broad implications for fundamental physics and potential advances in optics, wireless communications, and quantum science, benefiting society at large.
articles.personality_title: Scientists recreate black hole energy extraction in the lab using synthetic rotation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Over 50 years ago, physicists theorized that energy could be taken from spinning black holes. Sir Roger Penrose suggested that particles entering a black hole’s special region, called the ergosphere, could split so one piece escapes with more energy. Later, Yakov Zel'dovich predicted waves could also gain energy from fast rotation.

**What happened** – Researchers at the CUNY Advanced Science Research Center built a device that mimics ultrafast rotation without moving parts. Instead of spinning, the device rapidly changes its electrical properties in a ring, creating a pattern that tricks waves into thinking they are around a spinning object. This method successfully amplified waves and copied the energy extraction process from black holes.

**Impact** – This is the first time such extreme rotational physics has been recreated in a lab without mechanical motion. It turns a complex theory into a real experiment, offering a new way to study how waves interact with rotating systems. This approach could lead to improvements in technologies like wireless communication, optics (light control), and quantum science.

**What's next step** – Scientists plan to explore this synthetic rotation in other areas like photonics and quantum systems. They hope to develop new devices that control light and information more efficiently, inspired by the physics of black holes. More research is needed before these ideas become practical tools.

**One-sentence takeaway** – By creating synthetic ultrafast rotation in the lab, scientists have turned a decades-old black hole theory into a working experiment that could advance future technologies.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques recréent en laboratoire l'extraction d'énergie des trous noirs grâce à une rotation synthétique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Il y a plus de 50 ans, des physiciens ont imaginé qu'il serait possible d'extraire de l'énergie des trous noirs en rotation. Sir Roger Penrose a proposé qu'une particule entrant dans une région spéciale du trou noir, appelée ergosphère, puisse se diviser et qu'une partie s'échappe avec plus d'énergie. Plus tard, Yakov Zel'dovich a prédit que des ondes pouvaient aussi gagner de l'énergie grâce à une rotation rapide.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs du Centre avancé de recherche scientifique de CUNY ont construit un appareil qui imite une rotation ultrarapide sans pièces mobiles. Au lieu de tourner, l'appareil modifie rapidement ses propriétés électriques en anneau, créant un motif qui fait croire aux ondes qu'elles sont autour d'un objet en rotation. Cette méthode a amplifié les ondes en reproduisant le processus d'extraction d'énergie des trous noirs.

**Impact** – C'est la première fois que cette physique de rotation extrême est recréée en laboratoire sans mouvement mécanique. Cela transforme une théorie complexe en expérience réelle, offrant un nouveau moyen d'étudier l'interaction des ondes avec des systèmes en rotation. Cette approche pourrait améliorer des technologies comme les communications sans fil, l'optique (contrôle de la lumière) et la science quantique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent appliquer cette rotation synthétique à d'autres domaines comme la photonique et les systèmes quantiques. Ils espèrent développer de nouveaux dispositifs pour mieux contrôler la lumière et l'information, inspirés par la physique des trous noirs. D'autres recherches sont nécessaires avant de rendre ces idées pratiques.

**Résumé en une phrase** – En créant une rotation synthétique ultrarapide en laboratoire, les scientifiques ont transformé une théorie vieille de plusieurs décennies sur les trous noirs en une expérience fonctionnelle qui pourrait faire avancer les technologies futures.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos recrean en laboratorio la extracción de energía de agujeros negros usando rotación sintética
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Hace más de 50 años, físicos propusieron que se podía extraer energía de agujeros negros giratorios. Sir Roger Penrose sugirió que una partícula que entra en una región especial llamada ergosfera podría dividirse y que una parte escaparía con más energía. Luego, Yakov Zel'dovich predijo que las ondas también podrían ganar energía al interactuar con rotación rápida.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Centro Avanzado de Investigación Científica de CUNY construyeron un dispositivo que imita una rotación ultrarrápida sin partes móviles. En vez de girar, el dispositivo cambia rápidamente sus propiedades eléctricas en un anillo, creando un patrón que hace que las ondas crean que están alrededor de un objeto giratorio. Esto amplificó las ondas y reprodujo el proceso de extracción de energía de agujeros negros.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que esta física de rotación extrema se recrea en un laboratorio sin movimiento mecánico. Esto convierte una teoría compleja en un experimento real, ofreciendo una nueva forma de estudiar cómo las ondas interactúan con sistemas giratorios. Este método puede mejorar tecnologías como comunicaciones inalámbricas, óptica (control de la luz) y ciencia cuántica.

**Próximos pasos** – Los científicos planean usar esta rotación sintética en otras áreas como la fotónica y sistemas cuánticos. Esperan crear nuevos dispositivos que controlen mejor la luz y la información, inspirados en la física de los agujeros negros. Se necesita más investigación antes de que estas ideas se vuelvan herramientas prácticas.

**Resumen en una frase** – Al crear rotación sintética ultrarrápida en el laboratorio, los científicos han convertido una teoría de hace décadas sobre agujeros negros en un experimento funcional que podría impulsar tecnologías futuras.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing circular ring made of interconnected, softly illuminated resonators emitting gentle waves spiraling outward, set against a dark cosmic background with subtle swirling patterns evoking a spinning black hole’s ergosphere, symbolizing synthetic ultrafast rotation and energy amplification without physical motion.
